abstract.t: This document defines the Agent Credential Attestation Protocol (ACAP), a cryptographic credentialing protocol for autonomous AI agent pipelines. An ACAP credential is a short-lived JSON Web Token (JWT) signed with RS256 that carries scope-limited permissions together with a SHA-256 hash of the original human instruction that initiated the task. Credentials may be delegated to child agents; each delegation narrows scope, cannot outlive its parent, increments a delegation depth counter, and extends a tamper-evident chain of token identifiers. Every lifecycle event is recorded in an append-only, hash-chained audit log.
This document specifies the credential format, issuance rules, delegation rules, verification algorithm, revocation semantics, human-in-the-loop approval protocol, and audit log structure.

section.name: Problem Statement
section.t: Contemporary authorization frameworks such as OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect were designed for human-initiated, single-hop delegations: a resource owner grants a client access on behalf of themselves. AI agent pipelines violate this assumption in several important ways.

First, an agent pipeline may involve an arbitrary number of hops. A root agent receives a task from a human, decomposes it, and delegates sub-tasks to child agents, which may themselves delegate further. OAuth's two-party model has no native representation for this; each hop requires a fresh grant cycle or an out-of-band trust agreement.

Second, the originating human instruction -- the intent -- is not cryptographically bound to any OAuth token. An agent can receive a token whose original purpose has been transformed or corrupted by prompt injection at an intermediate step, and the verifying party has no way to detect this.

Third, scope in OAuth is flat and is not guaranteed to narrow monotonically through a delegation chain. A child agent can, in principle, present its parent token to a different authorization server and request broader access. Nothing in the wire format prevents scope creep.
Fourth, the delegation graph is not natively recorded. OAuth introspection surfaces information about a single token; it provides no view of the full task ancestry.

Recent IETF work -- notably the WIMSE working group and the draft on AI agents acting on behalf of users -- acknowledges these gaps but stops short of specifying a compact, self-contained credential format with cryptographically enforced monotone scope reduction and intent binding.

section.name: What ACAP Adds
section.t: ACAP addresses the gaps above as follows.

dd.t: Every ACAP credential carries att_intent, a hex-encoded SHA-256 hash of the original UTF-8 instruction text. This value is set at issuance and propagated unchanged through every delegation. A verifier can confirm that a presented credential descends from a specific human instruction by independently computing the hash.

At delegation time the issuer MUST verify that the child's requested scope is a subset of the parent's scope using the IsSubset algorithm defined in . Any request that fails this check MUST be rejected. Scope therefore only ever narrows; it can never widen through delegation.

Each credential carries att_depth, an integer that starts at 0 for a root credential and increments by 1 at each delegation. Depth is bounded above by MaxDelegationDepth (10). A credential whose depth equals this limit MUST NOT be used as a parent for further delegation.

Each credential carries att_chain, an ordered list of JWT IDs from the root of the delegation tree to the current token. The verifier checks that the chain length equals att_depth + 1 and that the final element equals the token's own jti. Together these checks detect any tampering with the ancestry record.

A child credential's expiry is capped at the parent's expiry. Concretely, exp = min(requested_exp, parent_exp). This ensures that revoking a parent token effectively expires all descendants, even without an active revocation lookup.

The revocation store records revoked JTIs and cascades revocation to all descendants by inspecting the att_chain column in the credential store. Revocation is permanent and takes precedence over token expiry.

Every issuance, delegation, verification, revocation, and expiry event is recorded in a hash-chained log. Each entry commits to the previous entry's hash, the event type, the JTI, and the timestamp, forming a tamper-evident record of all credential lifecycle events within a task tree.

section.name: Relationship to Prior Art
section.t: ACAP is informed by but is not a profile of any existing specification. The Agentic JWT (A-JWT) paper proposes delegation chains for AI agents but does not specify exact claim semantics, scope subset enforcement, or audit log structure. The IETF draft covers similar ground in the JWT format domain. The draft addresses delegation in the OAuth authorization code flow context. ACAP occupies a different point in the design space: it is a self-contained signed-token format with no external authorization server required at verification time.

section.name: Conventions and Definitions
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

dd.t: The service that creates and signs ACAP credentials. The Issuer holds an RSA private key and is identified by a URI recorded in the iss JWT claim. A single Issuer MAY serve multiple task trees.

An ACAP credential whose att_depth is 0 and whose att_pid is absent. A root credential is issued directly by the Issuer in response to a human principal's instruction. Its att_chain contains exactly one element: its own jti.

An ACAP credential whose att_depth is greater than 0. A delegated credential is derived from a parent credential and MUST have att_pid set to the parent's jti. Its scope MUST be a subset of the parent's scope and its expiry MUST NOT exceed the parent's expiry.
The complete graph of credentials that share the same att_tid. A task tree has exactly one root credential; all other credentials in the tree are delegated credentials descended from it.

The value of the att_intent claim: a lowercase hex-encoded SHA-256 digest of the UTF-8 encoding of the original human instruction string. The intent hash is set at root issuance and propagated unchanged to all descendants.
The integer value of att_depth. Depth 0 identifies a root credential. Each delegation increments depth by exactly 1. The maximum permitted depth is MaxDelegationDepth (10).

The ordered list of JWT IDs in att_chain. For a credential at depth D, the chain contains exactly D+1 elements: the root credential's jti at index 0, followed by each intermediate credential's jti in delegation order, with the current credential's jti at the final position (index D).
A string of the form resource:action where resource and action are non-empty strings. The wildcard character * MAY appear in either position.

section.name: Overview
section.t: An ACAP credential is a JSON Web Token with:

list.t: Header: { "alg": "RS256", "typ": "JWT" }
Payload: the claims defined in and
Signature: RS256 over the ASCII representation of the header and payload

section.name: Standard JWT Claims
section.t: The following standard JWT claims are used. All are REQUIRED unless noted.

texttable.c: Subject. MUST be of the form agent:{agent_id}
JWT ID. A UUID v4 that uniquely identifies this credential

section.t: The sub claim MUST match the pattern ^agent:[A-Za-z0-9_\-]+$. Implementations MUST reject credentials whose sub does not begin with the literal prefix agent:.

section.name: ACAP Extension Claims
section.t: All ACAP-specific claims are prefixed with att_. Implementations MUST ignore unknown att_* claims to allow forward compatibility.

texttable.c: SHA-256 of original instruction (64 hex chars)
Ordered list of JTIs from root to current
Identity of the human who approved
IdP issuer of the approving human
IdP issuer from root OIDC session
IdP subject from root OIDC session

section.t: att_pid MUST be present when att_depth > 0 and MUST be absent when att_depth == 0.
att_tid, att_intent, and att_uid MUST be propagated unchanged through all delegations.

section.name: Root Credential Payload
section.t: Note that att_pid is absent because this is a root credential. The att_chain contains exactly one element -- the credential's own jti.

section.name: Scope Entry Format
section.t: A scope entry is a string conforming to the grammar:

figure.artwork: scope-entry = resource ":" action resource = 1*( ALPHA / DIGIT / "_" / "-" / "*" ) action = 1*( ALPHA / DIGIT / "_" / "-" / "*" )

section.t: Both resource and action MUST be non-empty. Implementations MUST reject invalid scope entries at all stages: issuance, delegation, and verification.

section.name: Wildcard Rules
section.t: The wildcard character * MAY appear as the entirety of either the resource or action component (or both). Coverage rules:

list.t: P covers C if P.resource equals * OR P.resource equals C.resource
AND P.action equals * OR P.action equals C.action

section.t: Consequently *:* covers every valid scope entry, email:* covers email:read, email:draft, etc.

section.name: NormaliseScope
section.t: Before storing scope in any credential or comparing scopes, implementations MUST apply the NormaliseScope procedure:

list.t: Trim leading and trailing whitespace from each entry.
Remove any empty entries that result from trimming.
Remove duplicate entries, preserving the first occurrence.
Return the resulting list in insertion order.

section.name: Overview
section.t: Issuance creates a root credential (depth 0) that anchors a new task tree. The Issuer MUST perform all validation checks before constructing the credential. The Issuer MUST sign the credential with its RSA private key using RS256.

section.name: Input Validation
section.t: The Issuer MUST reject the issuance request if any of the following conditions hold:

list.t: agent_id is absent or empty.
user_id is absent or empty.
scope is absent or contains no entries.
Any entry in scope is not parseable as a valid scope entry.
instruction is absent or empty.

section.name: Intent Hash Computation
section.t: The intent hash is computed as:

The instruction string MUST be encoded as UTF-8 before hashing. The resulting 32-byte SHA-256 digest MUST be encoded as 64 lowercase hexadecimal characters. No canonicalization is applied to the instruction before hashing; the raw UTF-8 bytes are hashed as-is.

section.name: Identifier Generation
section.t: The Issuer MUST generate two UUID v4 values at issuance:

list.t: jti: the unique identifier for this credential.
att_tid: the task tree identifier.

section.t: Both values MUST be generated using a cryptographically secure random source.

list.t: If ttl_seconds is zero, the TTL defaults to DefaultTTLSeconds (3600 seconds).
If ttl_seconds is negative, the issuance MUST be rejected.
If ttl_seconds exceeds MaxTTLSeconds (86400 seconds), the TTL is capped at MaxTTLSeconds.
exp = iat + ttl_seconds (after applying the rules above).

section.name: Overview
section.t: Delegation creates a child credential derived from an existing parent credential. The result is a credential at att_depth + 1 with a scope that is a subset of the parent's scope and an expiry no later than the parent's expiry.

section.name: Input Validation
section.t: The Issuer MUST reject a delegation request if:

list.t: parent_token is absent or empty.
child_agent is absent or empty.
child_scope is absent or contains no entries.

section.name: Parent Token Verification
section.t: The Issuer MUST:

list.t: Verify the RS256 signature against the Issuer's public key.
Verify that the parent token has not expired (exp > now).
Verify that the signing algorithm is RS256.

section.name: Scope Subset Enforcement
section.t: The Issuer MUST apply NormaliseScope to the requested child_scope. The Issuer MUST then apply IsSubset(parentScope, childScope). If IsSubset returns false, the delegation MUST be rejected.

section.name: Depth Limit
section.t: The Issuer MUST check that parent.att_depth < MaxDelegationDepth (10). If the parent's depth equals or exceeds the limit, the delegation MUST be rejected.

section.name: Expiry Computation

figure.artwork:
parent_exp = parent.exp
if ttl_seconds > 0: requested_exp = now + ttl_seconds child_exp = min(requested_exp, parent_exp) else: default_exp = now + DefaultTTLSeconds child_exp = min(default_exp, parent_exp)

section.t: The child's expiry MUST NOT exceed the parent's expiry.

section.name: Overview
section.t: A credential is valid if and only if all of the following conditions are satisfied:

list.t: The RS256 signature verifies against the Issuer's public key.
The current time is before exp (subject to clock-skew allowance).
The length of att_chain equals att_depth + 1.
The final element of att_chain equals jti.
The jti is not present in the revocation store.

section.name: Warnings vs. Hard Failures
section.t: Chain length and chain tail inconsistencies are surfaced as warnings that cause the credential to be reported as invalid. The warning mechanism exists to distinguish between cryptographic failures (which may indicate active attack) and structural inconsistencies (which may indicate implementation bugs).

section.name: Semantics
section.t: Revocation permanently invalidates a credential identified by its jti. Once revoked, a credential MUST be treated as invalid regardless of its exp claim. Revocation is irreversible.

section.name: Cascade Semantics
section.t: Revoking a credential with jti X automatically revokes every credential whose att_chain contains X:
Cascade revocation SHOULD be performed atomically within a single database transaction.

section.name: Revocation Store
section.t: The revocation store records:

texttable.c: Agent or user ID that triggered revocation

section.t: Duplicate revocation attempts MUST be treated as a no-op (idempotent).

section.name: Lifetime Interaction
section.t: A credential that has expired is invalid regardless of the revocation store. A credential that has been revoked is invalid regardless of whether it has expired. These are independent failure conditions.

section.name: Overview
section.t: Certain delegations require explicit human approval before being granted. ACAP defines an approval protocol that makes the human decision a cryptographic event embedded in the resulting credential.

section.name: Approval Lifecycle
section.t: The HITL approval flow consists of four phases:

list.t: Request. The agent sends an approval request containing the parent_token, requested child_scope, an intent description, and the agent_id. The Issuer creates a pending approval record and returns a challenge_id.
Poll. The agent polls for approval status using the challenge_id. Status is one of: pending, approved, rejected, or expired.

Grant or Deny. A human reviews the request via a dashboard or integration. To grant, the human authenticates via an OIDC Identity Provider and the Issuer verifies the id_token. The Issuer records the human's identity and marks the approval as granted.
Credential Issuance. Upon grant, the Issuer delegates a new credential from the parent token with the approved scope. The credential carries att_hitl_req, att_hitl_uid, and att_hitl_iss.

section.name: Approval Expiry
section.t: Pending approvals MUST expire after a bounded time window. Implementations MUST NOT allow approvals to remain pending indefinitely. An expired approval MUST be treated identically to a rejection.

section.name: Parent Token Re-verification
section.t: When an approval is granted, the Issuer MUST re-verify the parent token before issuing the delegated credential. If the parent token has expired or been revoked while the approval was pending, the Issuer MUST reject the grant.

section.name: HITL Claims Propagation
section.t: The HITL claims (att_hitl_req, att_hitl_uid, att_hitl_iss) from the most recent human approval propagate to all subsequent delegations. If a new HITL approval occurs at a deeper delegation, the new claims replace the inherited ones.

section.name: Multi-Tenant Isolation
section.t: Approval requests are scoped to the authenticated organization. An approval created by org A MUST NOT be resolvable by org B.

section.name: Structure
section.t: The audit log is an append-only, hash-chained record of credential lifecycle events. Each entry commits to the previous entry's hash, making the log tamper-evident.
The audit log is partitioned by att_tid. Each task tree has its own independent hash chain. The first event uses a genesis hash of 64 ASCII zero characters as the previous hash value.

section.name: Entry Hash Computation

figure.artwork: entry_hash = lowercase-hex( SHA-256( prev_hash || event_type || jti || created_at_rfc3339nano ) )

section.t: where || denotes string concatenation. The genesis hash is:
(64 ASCII zero characters.)

texttable.c: Credential revoked (one event per cascade target)
Hash of the prior entry for same att_tid
One of the defined event types
JWT ID of the credential involved
Scope array at time of event

section.name: Append-Only Enforcement
section.t: Implementations MUST enforce append-only semantics on the audit log.

section.name: Log Verification
section.t: To verify the integrity of the audit log for a given att_tid:

list.t: Retrieve all entries in ascending id order.
For each entry starting at the second, verify that entry.prev_hash equals the entry_hash of the preceding entry.
Recompute entry_hash from the raw fields and verify it matches.

section.t: Any discrepancy indicates tampering or corruption.

section.name: Prompt Injection
section.t: ACAP credentials bind to the intent hash of the original instruction, but they do not prevent a legitimate credential from being used by a compromised agent. The narrow, monotone-reducing scope model limits the blast radius of a compromised agent. The intent hash enables post-hoc detection of misuse. The att_ack claim carries a checksum of the agent binary for agent substitution detection.

section.name: Replay Attacks
section.t: Credentials carry a unique jti. Verifiers MUST check the revocation store on every verification call. The exp claim bounds the replay window for non-revoked credentials. Verifiers SHOULD maintain a short-term cache of recently seen jti values.

section.name: Scope Creep
section.t: Scope creep is prevented by IsSubset enforcement at delegation time. Resource servers MUST verify that the presented credential's att_scope covers the requested operation.

section.name: Clock Skew
section.t: Implementations SHOULD allow a clock-skew leeway of up to 60 seconds. Implementations MUST NOT allow a leeway of more than 300 seconds.

section.name: Key Management
section.t: The Issuer's RSA private key is the root of trust. Compromise allows forging credentials. Implementations SHOULD use hardware security modules and short key rotation periods. The public key MUST be distributed via an out-of-band mechanism (e.g., a JWKS endpoint).

section.name: Credential Store Integrity
section.t: The revocation store and credential store are security-critical. Implementations MUST apply access controls preventing unauthorized modification.

section.name: Audit Log Integrity
section.t: Hash-chaining provides tamper-evidence but not tamper-prevention. The audit log SHOULD be replicated to a write-once store or transparency log for stronger guarantees.

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This specification defines the following JWT claim names for registration in the IANA "JSON Web Token Claims" registry established by .

front.title: Key words for use in RFCs to Indicate Requirement Levels

abstract.t: In many standards track documents several words are used to signify the requirements in the specification. These words are often capitalized. This document defines these words as they should be interpreted in IETF documents. This document specifies an Internet Best Current Practices for the Internet Community, and requests discussion and suggestions for improvements.

JSON Web Token (JWT) is a compact, URL-safe means of representing claims to be transferred between two parties. The claims in a JWT are encoded as a JSON object that is used as the payload of a JSON Web Signature (JWS) structure or as the plaintext of a JSON Web Encryption (JWE) structure, enabling the claims to be digitally signed or integrity protected with a Message Authentication Code (MAC) and/or encrypted.

This specification registers cryptographic algorithms and identifiers to be used with the JSON Web Signature (JWS), JSON Web Encryption (JWE), and JSON Web Key (JWK) specifications. It defines several IANA registries for these identifiers.

The OAuth 2.0 authorization framework enables a third-party application to obtain limited access to an HTTP service, either on behalf of a resource owner by orchestrating an approval interaction between the resource owner and the HTTP service, or by allowing the third-party application to obtain access on its own behalf. This specification replaces and obsoletes the OAuth 1.0 protocol described in RFC 5849. [STANDARDS-TRACK]

This specification defines UUIDs (Universally Unique IDentifiers) -- also known as GUIDs (Globally Unique IDentifiers) -- and a Uniform Resource Name namespace for UUIDs. A UUID is 128 bits long and is intended to guarantee uniqueness across space and time. UUIDs were originally used in the Apollo Network Computing System (NCS), later in the Open Software Foundation's (OSF's) Distributed Computing Environment (DCE), and then in Microsoft Windows platforms.

This specification is derived from the OSF DCE specification with the kind permission of the OSF (now known as "The Open Group"). Information from earlier versions of the OSF DCE specification have been incorporated into this document. This document obsoletes RFC 4122.

front.title: Ambiguity of Uppercase vs Lowercase in RFC 2119 Key Words

abstract.t: RFC 2119 specifies common key words that may be used in protocol specifications. This document aims to reduce the ambiguity by clarifying that only UPPERCASE usage of the key words have the defined special meanings.

front.title: Agentic JWT: Secure Delegation Protocol for AI Agent Pipelines
OAuth 2.0 for AI Agents Acting on Behalf of Users
IETF Workload Identity in Multi System Environments (WIMSE) Working Group

texttable.c: Previous hash for first audit entry

section.name: Implementation Status
section.t: A reference implementation is available as open source software:

list.t: Server: Go, available at https://github.com/chudah1/attest-dev
TypeScript SDK: @attest-dev/sdk on npm (0.1.0-beta.4)
Python SDK: attest-sdk on PyPI (0.1.0b3)
Framework integrations: Anthropic Claude, LangGraph, OpenAI Agents SDK, Model Context Protocol (MCP)
Deployment: Docker Compose, Railway

section.t: All SDKs implement credential issuance, delegation, offline verification via JWKS, revocation, and audit trail retrieval.

section.name: Data Model
section.t: The following SQL schema is informative for implementations using relational storage:

section.name: Acknowledgements
section.t: The authors acknowledge the contributions of the broader AI safety and identity communities, including the IETF WIMSE working group, the NIST AI Agent Standards Initiative, and the authors of the Agentic JWT proposal.
